meta.title: How do heat waves affect your body?
meta.speaker: Carolyn Beans
meta.youtubeTitle: How does extreme heat affect your body? - Carolyn Beans

sentences.text: The year is 2050 and your morning is not off to a good start.
sentences.translation: 这一年是 2050年，而你的早晨 却不是一个很好的开始。

sentences.text: School is closed for yet another heat day, meaning the kids need to stay home and the AC needs to stay on.
sentences.translation: 学校又因为高温停课，意味着孩子们得待在家里， 空调也要一直开着。

sentences.text: Your usual babysitter can’t come help because the rails for their commuter train were warped by the heat.
sentences.translation: 你平常的保姆不能来帮忙，因为高温把她通勤火车的铁轨 都烤变形了。

sentences.text: And to make matters worse, your dog is desperate for a walk, but the pavement is hot enough to give third degree burns to any paw or person that touches it.
sentences.translation: 更糟糕的是， 你的狗迫切希望散步，但地面热得足以让任何 接触到它的爪子或是行人，烫出三度烧伤。

sentences.text: In many parts of the world, this sweltering future is already here.
sentences.translation: 在世界的许多地方， 这样炎热的未来已经成为现实。

sentences.text: On average, heat waves are happening more often with greater intensity and for longer durations.
sentences.translation: 平均而言，热浪发生的频率 越来越高，强度越来越大，持续的时间也越来越长。

sentences.text: But according to a 2022 projection, by 2050, Earth’s mid-latitudes could be experiencing extreme heat between 90 and 180 days a year, with tropical regions enduring even more.
sentences.translation: 但是根据2022年的预测，到2050年，地球中纬度地区 每年可能会经历90到180天的极端高温，热带地区的高温天数甚至更多。

sentences.text: So, how hot is too hot, and what can people do to handle the heat?
sentences.translation: 那么到底多热才算太热呢？ 人们该如何应对高温呢？

sentences.text: While human bodies are decent at managing temperature, our cooling mechanisms only work under the right conditions.
sentences.translation: 虽然人体在调节体温方面还算不错，但我们的散热机制只能在 适当的条件下发挥作用。

sentences.text: When air temperatures climb, the hypothalamus tells blood vessels near the skin to widen, allowing more blood to flow near the body's surface and release heat.
sentences.translation: 当气温升高时，下丘脑会告诉皮肤附近的血管扩张，让更多的血液流向 人体表面附近并释放热量。

sentences.text: This hormonal cascade also turns on our sweat glands.
sentences.translation: 这种荷尔蒙级联反应也会 激发我们的汗腺。

sentences.text: As sweat evaporates, it pulls the heat from our skin.
sentences.translation: 当汗水蒸发时， 它会从皮肤带走热量。

sentences.text: But if humidity is high, the rate of evaporation slows and eventually stops.
sentences.translation: 但是，如果湿度过高， 蒸发的速度会减慢并最终停止。

sentences.text: Scientists use this principle to track humidity with a metric called wet-bulb temperature, in which they wrap a wet, room temperature cloth around a thermometer to see if evaporation will lower the reading.
sentences.translation: 科学家们利用这一原理， 采用了一种指标来追踪湿度，把它叫做“湿球温度”。他们用一块常温的湿布包裹温度计，

sentences.text: If it doesn't, it's too humid for sweat to cool us off.
sentences.translation: 看蒸发是否会降低读数。

sentences.text: A wet-bulb temperature of roughly 35°C is generally considered the limit of human survival, though current temperatures rarely reach this threshold.
sentences.translation: 如果没有降低，说明湿度太高， 汗水无法帮我们降温。湿球温度大约达到35°C时，会普遍被认为是人类生存的极限，

sentences.text: The US National Weather Service uses the relationship between humidity and air temperature as the basis for their heat index.
sentences.translation: 尽管目前的气温很少达到这一极限。美国国家气象局使用 湿度和气温之间的关系

sentences.text: As those two metrics rise, so too does the heat index; and heat is considered dangerous if the index climbs above 39.4°C.
sentences.translation: 作为热指数的基础。当这两个指标上升时， 热指数也会上升；如果指数超过 39.4°C，

sentences.text: That’s 103°F.
sentences.translation: 就被认为是危险高温。

sentences.text: But even a lower heat index can be hazardous over multiple days.
sentences.translation: 这相当于 103 华氏度。

sentences.text: A heat wave is a streak of two or more days of unusually hot weather for a place and season.
sentences.translation: 但即便是较低的热指数，持续多天 也可能是有害的。热浪是指在某地某个季节 连续两天或以上

sentences.text: For example, a string of 32°C days in Houston, Texas, is standard in the summer, but would constitute a heat wave in March.
sentences.translation: 出现异常高温的天气。例如，在德克萨斯州的休斯顿， 32°C的连续高温在夏季很常见，

sentences.text: And the impact of these events touches nearly every aspect of daily life.
sentences.translation: 但如果出现在三月， 就是热浪的形成。

sentences.text: Imagine a June heat wave strikes a tropical city.
sentences.translation: 这些事件的影响几乎涉及到 日常生活的方方面面。

sentences.text: The first to experience effects are outdoor workers.
sentences.translation: 想象一下，一场六月的热浪 袭击了整个热带城市。

sentences.text: Their excessive sweating leads to dehydration and muscle pain known as heat cramps.
sentences.translation: 首先感受到影响的是户外工作者。他们出汗过多会 导致脱水和肌肉疼痛，

sentences.text: If they push on, their conditions could worsen to heat exhaustion and even heat stroke— a life-threatening ailment that occurs when a body’s temperature exceeds 40°C.
sentences.translation: 也就是所谓的热痉挛。如果他们继续扛下去， 情况可能会恶化导致中暑，甚至是热射病 --

sentences.text: Medical emergency calls spike across the city, often for children and people who are pregnant or elderly.
sentences.translation: 这是一种当人体温度超过 40度时发生的危及生命的疾病。整个城市的医疗紧急电话激增，

sentences.text: The heat also increases hospital visits for heart, kidney, and lung-related conditions, creating an influx of patients that threatens to overwhelm medical providers.
sentences.translation: 通常是为儿童、孕妇或老人拨打的。高温还会增加因心脏、肾脏和肺部疾病而就诊的人数，

sentences.text: Over the following week, the city slows to a crawl.
sentences.translation: 从而导致患者大量涌入， 让医务人员不堪重负。

sentences.text: Schools and construction sites close.
sentences.translation: 在接下来的一周里， 整个城市陷入停滞状态。

sentences.text: Airplanes need to reduce their weight limits to take off, bumping countless travelers from their flights.
sentences.translation: 学校和建筑工地关闭。飞机必须减少起飞时的重量限制，

sentences.text: Restaurants shut down as overheated kitchens become unbearable.
sentences.translation: 导致无数旅客被迫取消航班。

sentences.text: Residents who remain inside with air conditioners stay safe.
sentences.translation: 过热的厨房让人难以忍受， 餐馆纷纷停业。

sentences.text: But blasting AC isn’t cheap, and many families have to choose between keeping cool and staying fed.
sentences.translation: 在室内使用空调的居民 虽能保持安全，但是老吹空调并不便宜，

sentences.text: Either way, if the heat continues, the stress of these air conditioners could overwhelm the power grid, potentially leading to city-wide outages.
sentences.translation: 许多家庭不得不在保持凉爽 和维持温饱之间做出选择。无论如何，如果高温持续下去，大量使用空调所致的压力 可能会使电网不堪重负，

sentences.text: These consequences are all very real.
sentences.translation: 甚至导致全城停电。

sentences.text: Each year, close to 500,000 people die due to excessive heat, and these extreme conditions are only growing more common.
sentences.translation: 这些后果都是非常真实的。每年有近50万人死于过热，

sentences.text: We can limit medical impacts by seeking help for heat-related illnesses, staying hydrated, and keeping people cool through public access to water and AC.
sentences.translation: 而这些极端情况 正在变得越来越常见。我们可以通过寻求 与过热相关疾病的帮助、保持水分、

sentences.text: But don’t let anyone tell you 1 to 2 degrees doesn’t matter.
sentences.translation: 以及通过使用公共供水、空调 让人们保持凉爽来控制疾病的影响。

sentences.text: It will change our very way of life.
sentences.translation: 但不要让任何人告诉你1到2度的 高温变化无关紧要，

cues.text: The year is 2050 and your morning is not off to a good start.
School is closed for yet another heat day,
meaning the kids need to stay home and the AC needs to stay on.
Your usual babysitter can’t come help
because the rails for their commuter train were warped by the heat.
And to make matters worse, your dog is desperate for a walk,
but the pavement is hot enough to give third degree burns
to any paw or person that touches it.
In many parts of the world, this sweltering future is already here.
On average, heat waves are happening more often with greater intensity
and for longer durations.
But according to a 2022 projection,
by 2050, Earth’s mid-latitudes could be experiencing extreme heat
between 90 and 180 days a year,
with tropical regions enduring even more.
So, how hot is too hot, and what can people do to handle the heat?
While human bodies are decent at managing temperature,
our cooling mechanisms only work under the right conditions.
When air temperatures climb,
the hypothalamus tells blood vessels near the skin to widen,
allowing more blood to flow near the body's surface and release heat.
This hormonal cascade also turns on our sweat glands.
As sweat evaporates, it pulls the heat from our skin.
But if humidity is high, the rate of evaporation slows and eventually stops.
Scientists use this principle to track humidity with a metric
called wet-bulb temperature,
in which they wrap a wet, room temperature cloth around a thermometer
to see if evaporation will lower the reading.
If it doesn't, it's too humid for sweat to cool us off.
A wet-bulb temperature of roughly 35°C
is generally considered the limit of human survival,
though current temperatures rarely reach this threshold.
The US National Weather Service uses the relationship between humidity
and air temperature as the basis for their heat index.
As those two metrics rise, so too does the heat index;
and heat is considered dangerous
if the index climbs above 39.4°C.
That’s 103°F.
But even a lower heat index can be hazardous over multiple days.
A heat wave is a streak of two or more days of unusually hot weather
for a place and season.
For example, a string of 32°C days in Houston, Texas,
is standard in the summer,
but would constitute a heat wave in March.
And the impact of these events touches nearly every aspect of daily life.
Imagine a June heat wave strikes a tropical city.
The first to experience effects are outdoor workers.
Their excessive sweating leads to dehydration and muscle pain
known as heat cramps.
If they push on, their conditions could worsen
to heat exhaustion and even heat stroke—
a life-threatening ailment that occurs when a body’s temperature exceeds 40°C.
Medical emergency calls spike across the city,
often for children and people who are pregnant or elderly.
The heat also increases hospital visits for heart, kidney,
and lung-related conditions,
creating an influx of patients that threatens to overwhelm medical providers.
Over the following week, the city slows to a crawl.
Schools and construction sites close.
Airplanes need to reduce their weight limits to take off,
bumping countless travelers from their flights.
Restaurants shut down as overheated kitchens become unbearable.
Residents who remain inside with air conditioners stay safe.
But blasting AC isn’t cheap,
and many families have to choose between keeping cool and staying fed.
Either way, if the heat continues,
the stress of these air conditioners could overwhelm the power grid,
potentially leading to city-wide outages.
These consequences are all very real.
Each year, close to 500,000 people die due to excessive heat,
and these extreme conditions are only growing more common.
We can limit medical impacts by seeking help for heat-related illnesses,
staying hydrated,
and keeping people cool through public access to water and AC.
But don’t let anyone tell you 1 to 2 degrees doesn’t matter.
It will change our very way of life.

cuesZh.text: 这一年是 2050年，而你的早晨 却不是一个很好的开始。
学校又因为高温停课，
意味着孩子们得待在家里， 空调也要一直开着。
你平常的保姆不能来帮忙，
因为高温把她通勤火车的铁轨 都烤变形了。
更糟糕的是， 你的狗迫切希望散步，
但地面热得足以让任何 接触到它的爪子
或是行人，烫出三度烧伤。
在世界的许多地方， 这样炎热的未来已经成为现实。
平均而言，热浪发生的频率 越来越高，强度越来越大，
持续的时间也越来越长。
但是根据2022年的预测，
到2050年，地球中纬度地区 每年可能会经历
90到180天的极端高温，
热带地区的高温天数甚至更多。
那么到底多热才算太热呢？ 人们该如何应对高温呢？
虽然人体在调节体温方面还算不错，
但我们的散热机制只能在 适当的条件下发挥作用。
当气温升高时，
下丘脑会告诉皮肤附近的血管扩张，
让更多的血液流向 人体表面附近并释放热量。
这种荷尔蒙级联反应也会 激发我们的汗腺。
当汗水蒸发时， 它会从皮肤带走热量。
但是，如果湿度过高， 蒸发的速度会减慢并最终停止。
科学家们利用这一原理， 采用了一种指标来追踪湿度，
把它叫做“湿球温度”。
他们用一块常温的湿布
包裹温度计，
看蒸发是否会降低读数。
如果没有降低，说明湿度太高， 汗水无法帮我们降温。
湿球温度大约达到35°C时，
会普遍被认为是人类生存的极限，
尽管目前的气温很少达到这一极限。
美国国家气象局使用 湿度和气温之间的关系
作为热指数的基础。
当这两个指标上升时， 热指数也会上升；
如果指数超过 39.4°C，
就被认为是危险高温。
这相当于 103 华氏度。
但即便是较低的热指数，持续多天 也可能是有害的。
热浪是指在某地某个季节 连续两天或以上
出现异常高温的天气。
例如，在德克萨斯州的休斯顿， 32°C的连续高温
在夏季很常见，
但如果出现在三月， 就是热浪的形成。
这些事件的影响几乎涉及到 日常生活的方方面面。
想象一下，一场六月的热浪 袭击了整个热带城市。
首先感受到影响的是户外工作者。
他们出汗过多会 导致脱水和肌肉疼痛，
也就是所谓的热痉挛。
如果他们继续扛下去， 情况可能会恶化
导致中暑，甚至是热射病 --
这是一种当人体温度超过 40度时发生的危及生命的疾病。
整个城市的医疗紧急电话激增，
通常是为儿童、孕妇或老人拨打的。
高温还会增加因心脏、肾脏
和肺部疾病而就诊的人数，
从而导致患者大量涌入， 让医务人员不堪重负。
在接下来的一周里， 整个城市陷入停滞状态。
学校和建筑工地关闭。
飞机必须减少起飞时的重量限制，
导致无数旅客被迫取消航班。
过热的厨房让人难以忍受， 餐馆纷纷停业。
在室内使用空调的居民 虽能保持安全，
但是老吹空调并不便宜，
许多家庭不得不在保持凉爽 和维持温饱之间做出选择。
无论如何，如果高温持续下去，
大量使用空调所致的压力 可能会使电网不堪重负，
甚至导致全城停电。
这些后果都是非常真实的。
每年有近50万人死于过热，
而这些极端情况 正在变得越来越常见。
我们可以通过寻求 与过热相关疾病的帮助、
保持水分、
以及通过使用公共供水、空调 让人们保持凉爽来控制疾病的影响。
但不要让任何人告诉你1到2度的 高温变化无关紧要，
它会彻底改变我们的生活方式。
